title: Abolitionism 101
orientation: A pillar/overview page from Abolitionists Rising introducing the abolitionist movement against abortion, contrasting its five tenets with the Pro-Life Movement and inviting readers to join. Aimed at Christians, pro-lifers, and curious newcomers trying to understand what abolitionism is and how it differs from mainstream pro-life activism.
audiences: Christian seeking a biblical framework on abortion

questions.question: What is abolitionism and how is it different from being pro-life?
questions.answer: Abolitionists hold that abortion is murder and must be completely abolished in the name of Jesus Christ, not merely regulated or reduced. Although many pro-lifers also call abortion murder, the Pro-Life Movement has not opposed it in a way consistent with that conviction. The article frames the divide as fundamentally theological, not just strategic.
questions.quote: We, as an abolitionist organization, agree with the pro-life position that abortion is bad, but further, we believe that abortion is murder and that it ought to be completely abolished as murder in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ.

questions.question: Why do abolitionists say the difference with the Pro-Life Movement is theological?
questions.audience: Christian seeking a biblical framework on abortion
questions.answer: Abolitionists see the gap not as a tactical disagreement but as a difference in underlying theology, which then shapes very different actions on abortion. Because pro-life leaders operate from unbiblical theological commitments, their political behavior ends up being unbiblical too.
questions.quote: The root of this difference between the Abolitionist Movement and Pro-Life Movement is theological.

questions.question: What are the five tenets of abolitionism?
questions.answer: Abolitionists describe themselves with five marks: Gospel-Centered, Providential, Church-Driven, Biblical, and Uncompromising. These foundations are presented as the core distinction between abolitionism and the Pro-Life Movement.

questions.question: How do abolitionists characterize the Pro-Life Movement?
questions.answer: The article describes the Pro-Life Movement as Secular, Worldly wise, Lobbyist-driven, and Compromising. These traits are positioned as the inverse of the abolitionist tenets and as the source of the movements' divergent actions.

questions.question: Which Bible passages do abolitionists cite as proof texts?
questions.audience: Christian seeking a biblical framework on abortion
questions.answer: An episode of The Liberator Podcast referenced in the article walks through abolitionist proof texts including 2 Corinthians 4, Exodus 23, Isaiah 1, 10, and 30, Acts 5, and 2 Kings 18. These are presented as the biblical scaffolding for the abolitionist case.
questions.quote: This episode of The Liberator Podcast addresses abolitionism proof texts like 2 Corinthians 4, Exodus 23, Isaiah 1, 10, & 30, Acts 5, & 2 Kings 18.

questions.question: What is an example of pro-life leaders opposing immediate abolition?
questions.answer: The article points to an open letter signed by 76 pro-life groups that killed the Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act. It is offered as evidence that the leadership of the Pro-Life Movement actively works against legislation that would immediately end abortion.
questions.quote: 76 pro-life groups signed an open letter which killed the Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act.

questions.question: Do pro-life leaders ever push for exceptions like rape and incest?
questions.answer: Yes. The article cites National Right to Life VP Tony Lauinger as the figure behind legislation to add rape and incest exceptions for abortion into Oklahoma state law. This is given as a particularly egregious example of pro-life leaders promoting injustice rather than just failing to oppose it.
questions.quote: As a particularly egregious example, National Right to Life VP Tony Lauinger is behind legislation to add rape and incest exceptions for abortion into Oklahoma State law.

questions.question: Why does the article say Christians should leave the Pro-Life Movement?
questions.audience: Christian seeking a biblical framework on abortion
questions.answer: Because the Pro-Life Establishment, in the article's view, calls good evil and evil good and is the chief obstacle to abolishing abortion in many states, Christians are urged to separate from it. The call is framed in the biblical language of coming out from among them and becoming abolitionists.
questions.quote: Add all of this up, and what you get is a Pro-Life Establishment that calls good evil and evil good; a Pro-Life Establishment that is the primary impediment to the abolition of abortion in many states. Accordingly, it is necessary for Christians to come out from among them and become abolitionists.

questions.question: How can I get involved in the abolitionist movement?
questions.answer: The article points readers to a petition as a concrete first step into the movement. It frames signing the petition as a great onramp for someone ready to join.
questions.quote: If you want to get join the Abolitionist Movement, a great first step is signing the petition!

questions.question: Where should I go if I have more questions about why abolitionists oppose the Pro-Life Movement?
questions.answer: The article directs readers to the companion page "Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life" for a fuller treatment of the differences between the two camps. That page is presented as the recommended next read for anyone wrestling with the distinction.
questions.quote: If you have more questions about why Abolitionists oppose the Pro-Life Movement, and want to know more about the difference between pro-life and abolitionist, start with our page: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Explicitly linked from this article as the deeper-dive on the abolitionist vs. pro-life distinction.

related.title: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
related.reason: Develops the theological roots that this overview names as the core difference with the Pro-Life Movement.

related.title: Biblical, Not Secular
related.reason: Expands the 'Biblical' tenet and the contrast with a 'Secular' Pro-Life Movement.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Pillar page for the 'Uncompromising' tenet and the case against incrementalism referenced via the Louisiana bill.

related.title: No Exceptions
related.reason: Directly addresses the rape-and-incest exceptions issue raised here through the Tony Lauinger example.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Fills in what abolitionist legislation that treats abortion as murder actually looks like in law.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Companion pillar on the political/legal mechanism abolitionists advocate for state-level abolition.
